title: Document Date

title: List of Tables Reference
summary: Reference to the list of 27 tables contained in the document, covering topics from major considerations to sensitivity issues.
source_quote: LIST OF TABLES ... Table 27. Sensitivity issues

title: 1. Introduction
summary: Defines the purpose and scope of the Timber Supply Review data package.
source_quote: 1. Introduction

title: Summary of Introduction
summary: The data package provides inputs for timber supply analysis based on 'current performance' (last 5-10 years), modeling 'what is' rather than 'what if'. It includes an explanation of data, data tables, and comment areas.
source_quote: The data package for the Timber Supply Review (TSR) program is simply an organized and consistent format for supplying the basic inputs required for a timber supply analysis.

title: Scope of Modeling
summary: The TSR models current performance ('what is') and excludes future changes in objectives or data.
source_quote: In other words, the purpose of the timber supply review program is to model 'what is' not 'what if'.

title: 2. Current Forest Management Considerations and Issues
summary: Outlines the base case assumptions and major management issues.
source_quote: 2. Current Forest Management Considerations and Issues

title: 2.1 Base case management assumptions
summary: Describes the assumptions reflecting current performance for the base case forecast.
source_quote: 2.1 Base case management assumptions

title: Base Case Assumptions Summary
summary: Assumptions reflect current forest land status, management practices, and growth/yield knowledge. These form the base case harvest forecast. Uncertainties are tested in Section 7.
source_quote: These assumptions reflect current performance with respect to the status of forest land, forest management practices and knowledge of timber growth and yield.

title: 2.2 Statement of major forest management considerations and issues
summary: Lists major forest management issues and their impact on timber supply.
source_quote: 2.2 Statement of major forest management considerations and issues

title: Table 1. Major forest management considerations
summary: Lists considerations including Land Use Plans, Strategic Resources, Biodiversity, Municipal Planning, Infrastructure, Water, Lead Reservoirs, and IRMPs with descriptions.
source_quote: Table 1. Major forest management considerations

title: Cariboo-Chilcotin Land Use Plan
summary: Government has accepted the CCLUP, reflected in higher-level plan orders.
source_quote: Government has accepted the Cariboo-Chilcotin Land Use Plan (CCLUP) and the objectives are reflected in a higher-level plan order.

title: Strategic Resources Management Plan
summary: SRMPs are spatial applications of CCLUP direction with 47 objectives guiding operational planners.
source_quote: Strategic Resources Management Plans (SRMP) are a spatial application of the CCLUP direction at the sub-regional planning level. There are 47 objectives in the plan to guide operational planners.

title: SRMP Objectives Count
summary: There are 47 objectives in the Strategic Resources Management Plan.
source_quote: There are 47 objectives in the plan to guide operational planners.

title: Landscape-level biodiversity
summary: CCLUP requires maintaining landscape-level biodiversity by meeting M+O and old forest objectives.
source_quote: The CCLUP requires that landscape-level biodiversity be maintained by meeting or exceeding mature plus old (M+O) and old forest objectives for each landscape unit (LU).

title: Stand-level biodiversity
summary: Chief forester released guidance on retention in MPB salvage operations in Dec 2005.
source_quote: In December 2005, the chief forester released guidance on landscape and stand-level structural retention in large-scale mountain pine beetle (MPB) salvage operations (Snetsinger 2005).

title: 100 Mile House District enhanced retention strategy
summary: Strategy released in 2007 to increase stand level retention during MPB salvage.
source_quote: In 2007, the 100 Mile House District released the District Enhanced Retention Strategy.

title: 3. Inventories
source_quote: 3. Inventories

title: 3. Inventories
summary: Data sources for the analysis include corporate level data (BCGW), Forest Analysis and Inventory Branch (FAIB) data, and local branch/region/district data. Exceptions include RESULTS1 and SIBEC.
source_quote: There are generally three sources of data for the analysis; corporate level data that resides in the provincial geographic data warehouse (BCGW), data maintained by the Forest Analysis and Inventory Branch (FAIB) and local data that is stored at the branch, region or district level.  Two  notable exceptions are RESULTS1 information, this is maintained by Resource Practices Branch, and SIBEC which is also maintained by the Ministry of Forests, Lands and Natural Resource Operations (FLNR).

title: 3.2 Forest cover inventory
source_quote: 3.2 Forest cover inventory

title: 3.2 Forest cover inventory
summary: The original forest cover inventory is from 1972/1976, updated annually. VRI Phase 1 was conducted in 1998 for IFPA areas. A recent VRI Phase 1 re-inventory is underway but not complete until Dec 2013. Current inventory vintages are mixed: 83% rolled over to VRI standards in 2003, 17% under IFPA.
source_quote: The original forest cover inventory for 100 Mile House TSA is from 1972 and 1976. ... Effectively, the current inventory is of two vintages since 83% original forest cover inventory has been rolled over to VRI standards in 2003 and 17% has a complete or incomplete VRI done under the IFPA.

title: Inventory Vintage Distribution
summary: 83% of the original forest cover inventory rolled over to VRI standards in 2003.
source_quote: 83% original forest cover inventory has been rolled over to VRI standards in 2003

title: IFPA Vintage Distribution
summary: 17% has a complete or incomplete VRI done under the IFPA.
source_quote: 17% has a complete or incomplete VRI done under the IFPA

title: Re-inventory Completion Date
summary: The VRI Phase 1 re-inventory will not be completed until December 2013.
source_quote: This will not be completed until December 2013

title: 3.3 Predictive ecosystem mapping
source_quote: 3.3 Predictive ecosystem mapping2

title: 3.3 Predictive ecosystem mapping
summary: PEM was completed in spring 2008. Accuracy assessment by CDT-Core Decision Technologies Inc. and Timberline Natural Resources Group determined 72.3% accuracy, exceeding the 65% requirement. It meets 2009 standards for base case timber supply analysis.
source_quote: The PEM for the 100 Mile House TSA was completed in the spring of 2008. ... The accuracy assessment determined map accuracy to be 72.3% (by Ministry of Environment standards), well in excess of the required 65%.  As a result, the PEM met the current (2009) standards for use in base case timber supply analysis.

title: PEM Accuracy
summary: Map accuracy assessed at 72.3%.
source_quote: map accuracy to be 72.3%

title: PEM Accuracy Requirement
summary: Required accuracy standard is 65%.
source_quote: required 65%

title: 4. Division of the Area into Management Zones
source_quote: 4. Division of the Area into Management Zones

title: 4.1 Management zones and tracking of multiple objectives
source_quote: 4.1 Management zones and tracking of multiple objectives

title: 4.1 Management zones and tracking of multiple objectives
summary: Management zones differentiate areas with distinct management emphasis (e.g., harvesting system, wildlife). Groups track multiple objectives over time. This section references Table 3 for objectives to be tracked.
source_quote: The concept of management zones is used to differentiate areas with distinct management emphasis. ... Grouping enables constraints to apply to different combinations of variables or zones ... Further information on the forest cover requirements to be applied to these areas can be found in Section 6.6, “Integrated Resource Management”.

title: 5. Timber Harvesting Land Base Definition
source_quote: 5. Timber Harvesting Land Base Definition

title: 5.1 Identification of the timber harvesting land base
source_quote: 5.1 Identification of the timber harvesting land base

title: 5.1 Identification of the timber harvesting land base
summary: This section defines the timber harvesting land base by excluding areas unavailable for harvesting due to administration, suitability, or availability constraints, and including areas added via management or acquisition. It lists 11 categories of exclusion such as private land, non-forest types, roads, and protected areas.
source_quote: This section outlines the steps used to identify the timber harvesting land base (the productive forest expected to support timber harvesting) within the timber supply area.

title: 5.2 Details on land base classification
source_quote: 5.2 Details on land base classification

title: 5.2.1 Land not administered by FLNR for timber supply purposes
source_quote: 5.2.1 Land not administered by FLNR for timber supply purposes

title: 5.2.1 Land not administered by FLNR for timber supply purposes
summary: Ownership codes determine eligibility for timber supply, with only codes 62C and 69C (Crown land) generally included. All other ownership codes, including woodlots, the Clinton Community Forest Agreement, and the Canim Lake First Nations Replaceable Forest Licence, are excluded from the analysis.
source_quote: Ownership codes are generally used to identify whether the land can be considered to contribute to timber supply. Ownership codes 62C and 69C indicate crown land in a forest management unit and miscellaneous reserves respectively.

title: 5.2.1.1 Woodlots
source_quote: 5.2.1.1 Woodlots

title: 5.2.1.1 Woodlots
summary: Woodlot allowable annual cuts are determined individually, so these lands are excluded from the timber supply analysis.
source_quote: Woodlot allowable annual cuts are determined individually; these lands are excluded from the analysis.

title: 5.2.1.2 Clinton Community Forest Agreement
source_quote: 5.2.1.2 Clinton Community Forest Agreement

title: 5.2.1.2 Clinton Community Forest Agreement
summary: The Clinton Community Forest Agreement is pending legal status and its AAC is determined separately, so it is excluded from the analysis.
source_quote: The Clinton Community Forest Agreement, which includes the Clinton Creek Community Watershed, is pending legal status. The AAC for the forest agreement will be determined under a separate process. These lands are excluded from the analysis.

title: 5.2.1.3 Canim Lake First Nations Replaceable Forest Licence
source_quote: 5.2.1.3 Canim Lake First Nations Replaceable Forest Licence

title: 5.2.1.3 Canim Lake First Nations Replaceable Forest Licence
summary: The Canim Lake First Nations Replaceable Forest Licence is pending legal status and excluded from the analysis, though a sensitivity test will examine its potential contribution to harvest opportunities.
source_quote: The Canim Lake First Nations Replaceable Forest Licence is pending legal status. The AAC for the forest licence will be determined under a separate process. These lands are excluded from the analysis.

title: 5.2.2 Land classified as non-forest
source_quote: 5.2.2 Land classified as non-forest

title: 5.2.2 Land classified as non-forest
summary: Non-forest areas (alpine, lakes, rocks) are removed from the land base. Areas not classified as Forest Management Land Base (FMLB) in the VRI are excluded to avoid misclassifying recently harvested areas.
source_quote: Non-forest areas such as alpine, lakes, rocks etc. are removed from the land base considered for timber supply.

title: Timber Harvesting Land Base Definition
summary: Section 5 introduces the definition of the timber harvesting land base, starting with sites of low timber growing potential.
source_quote: 5. Timber Harvesting Land Base Definition

title: Summary of Timber Harvesting Land Base Definition Introduction
summary: The section defines the timber harvesting land base, detailing reductions for low timber growing potential sites such as old-growth ponderosa pine, lodgepole pine, and interior Douglas-fir.
source_quote: Table 7. Description of sites with low timber growing potential... Reduction (%)... 50... 100

source_quote: Table 7. Description of sites with low timber growing potential... Reduction (%)... All Py All All All 50...

title: Reduction percentage for all sites
summary: A 50% reduction is applied to all sites for ponderosa pine.
source_quote: All Py All All All 50

title: Reduction percentage for low potential species
summary: A 100% reduction is applied to sites where lodgepole pine, interior Douglas-fir, spruce, subalpine fir, or deciduous species are older than specified ages and have low volume.
source_quote: All Pli > 80 N/A < 65 100... All Fdi, Sx/Se, Bl > 120 N/A < 65 100... All Deciduous > 120 N/A < 65 100

source_quote: Wildlife habitat reductions may be identified and managed through several processes... Table 8. Estimates for wildlife habitat excluded areas

summary: WHA 5-115 has a 100% exclusion rate for Mountain Caribou.

summary: WHA 5-073 has a 100% exclusion rate for a data-sensitive species.

summary: WHA 5-875 has a 100% exclusion rate for Badger.

summary: WHA 5-895 has a 100% exclusion rate for Great Basin Spadefoot.

section_path: 6. Current Forest Management Assumptions/6.1.2 Volume exclusions for mixed-species stands
title: 6.1.2 Volume exclusions for mixed-species stands
source_quote: 6.1.2 Volume exclusions for mixed-species stands

section_path: 6. Current Forest Management Assumptions/6.1.2 Volume exclusions for mixed-species stands
title: Volume exclusions for mixed-species stands
summary: This section addresses the exclusion of unmerchantable species in mixed-species stands from estimated stand volumes. While deciduous species may not be harvested, they may contribute to biodiversity. Table 12 details specific volume exclusions, indicating 0% exclusion for all deciduous in conifer-leading stands and 50% for all Poplar (Py) in certain contexts. Comments indicate hardwoods constitute a minor portion of volume (<1% of AAC) and are largely incidental harvests, though aspen is explicitly included in the analysis despite being <1% of volume.
source_quote: One or more species in mixed-species stands may be unmerchantable. ... The species that do not contribute will be excluded from merchantable stand volumes are shown in Table 12.

section_path: 6. Current Forest Management Assumptions/6.1.2 Volume exclusions for mixed-species stands
title: Table 12. Volume exclusions for mixed-species types
summary: Details volume exclusion percentages for mixed-species inventory types.
source_quote: Table 12. Volume exclusions for mixed-species types Inventory type group Species Volume exclusion (%) Conifer leading All deciduous 0 All Py 50

section_path: 6. Current Forest Management Assumptions/6.1.2 Volume exclusions for mixed-species stands
title: Hardwood volume percentage
summary: Data indicates hardwoods constitute approximately two percent of standing volume in mixed-pine leading stands and 0.43% of total volume tracked against annual cut in early 2011.
source_quote: hardwoods constitute approximately two percent of the standing volume in mixed-pine leading stands ... billed deciduous volume as 0.43% of the total volume

section_path: 6. Current Forest Management Assumptions/6.1.2 Volume exclusions for mixed-species stands
title: Inclusion of Aspen in TSR
summary: Aspen is included in the timber supply analysis even though it constitutes less than one percent of total harvest volume, differing from previous TSRs where it was fully excluded.
source_quote: aspen is a managed species within the TSA and ... it should be included in the timber supply analysis even though it constitutes less than one percent of the total harvest volume. This differs from previous TSR in which both ponderosa pine and deciduous species were fully excluded.

section_path: 6. Current Forest Management Assumptions/6.1.2 Volume exclusions for mixed-species stands
title: Sensitivity analysis for aspen exclusion
summary: Section 7 will examine the effects of fully excluding aspen from the timber supply analysis.
source_quote: Sensitivity analysis (Section 7) will examine the effects of full exclusion of aspen on the timber supply.

section_path: 6. Current Forest Management Assumptions/6.1.3 Minimum harvestable age criteria
title: 6.1.3 Minimum harvestable age criteria
source_quote: 6.1.3 Minimum harvestable age criteria

section_path: 6. Current Forest Management Assumptions/6.1.3 Minimum harvestable age criteria
title: Minimum harvestable age criteria
summary: Defines the minimum age and volume requirements for analysis units to be eligible for harvest. Table 13 specifies criteria: Pine stands require minimum age 12.5 years (referenced as 60-80 in footnote), height 65, diameter 60; Non-pine stands require minimum age 17.5 years (referenced as 80-100 in footnote), height 100, diameter 80. Comments note that optimal objectives are to avoid harvesting until culmination age, but criteria may be adjusted for flow objectives.
source_quote: The purpose of this section is to identify the minimum stand criteria an analysis unit must meet to be eligible for harvest. To become eligible for harvest both age and volume requirements must be met.

section_path: 6. Current Forest Management Assumptions/6.1.3 Minimum harvestable age criteria
title: Table 13. Minimum harvestable age criteria
summary: Lists minimum harvestable age, height, diameter, and volume criteria for Pine and Non-pine analysis units.
source_quote: Table 13. Minimum harvestable age criteria Analysis unit Minimum criteria Minimum harvest age (years) Height class Diameter Volume Pine All 12.5 65 60 10 Non-pine All 17.5 100 80 11

section_path: 6. Current Forest Management Assumptions/6.1.4 Harvest scheduling
title: 6.1.4 Harvest scheduling
source_quote: 6.1.4 Harvest scheduling

section_path: 6. Current Forest Management Assumptions/6.1.4 Harvest scheduling
title: Harvest scheduling priorities
summary: Describes the setting of priorities or harvest levels on management zones to reflect insect infestations, salvage operations, or other objectives. Table 14 outlines suggested priorities: Priority 1 includes beetle salvage in >70% Pine or Spruce/Lodgepole stands; Priority 2 is beetle salvage in 50-70% Pine; Priority 3 is conserving growing stock in mixed-risk spruce or all other stands. Comments emphasize maximizing salvage volumes and conserving green components to mitigate mid-term deficits.
source_quote: Table 14 describes suggested harvest scheduling priorities and limitations within the 100 Mile House TSA for use in the analysis.

title: 6. Current Forest Management Assumptions
summary: Section heading for current forest management assumptions.
source_quote: 6. Current Forest Management Assumptions

title: Single tree selection ungulate winter range modelling
summary: Assumptions regarding the modelling of single tree selection in shallow and moderate snowpack zones, including retention levels based on Douglas-fir percentage and understory regeneration factors.
source_quote: The GWM for shallow and moderate snowpack zones identifies five different types of stands and treatments... Stands with > 40% Douglas-fir will be modelled in the analysis under a clumpy single tree selection system.

title: Table 16a. Retention levels by habitat class
summary: Specifies retention percentages and minimum time between entries for Low, Moderate, and High stand structure habitat classes in single tree selection systems.
source_quote: Table 16a. Retention levels by habitat class ... Low 65 30 ... Moderate 75 30 ... High 85 30

title: Group selection ungulate winter range modelling
summary: Assumptions for modelling group selection in transition and deep snowpack zones, targeting a mosaic of age classes in stands with >40% Douglas-fir.
source_quote: The GWM for transition and deep snowpack zones identifies six different stands and treatments... Small patch or group selection is the desired treatment in stands having > 40% Douglas-fir with the goal of producing a mosaic of age classes across the landscape.

title: Table 16b. Harvest cycles by habitat class
summary: Defines harvest area, minimum cutting cycle, effective rotation period, and resulting stand age classes for Low, Moderate, and High habitat classes under group selection.
source_quote: Table 16b. Harvest cycles by habitat class ... Low 33 40 120 3 ... Moderate 25 40 160 4 ... High 20 40 200 5

title: Clearcut with reserves ungulate winter range modelling
summary: Stands with less than 40% Douglas-fir are modelled under a clearcut with reserve system, with a long-term goal to convert to multi-aged single tree selection.
source_quote: Stands with < 40% Douglas-fir will be modelled under a clearcut with reserve system as described above. The long-term goal is to convert these stands to the multi-aged single tree selection system.

title: 6.1.5.3 Caribou habitat
summary: Section heading for caribou habitat management assumptions.
source_quote: 6.1.5.3 Caribou habitat

title: Table 17. Caribou habitat harvest cycles
summary: Details the group selection silviculture system for Caribou WHA 5-116, including volume removal, number of entries, min time between entries, and effective rotation age.
source_quote: Table 17. Caribou habitat harvest cycles ... Group selection Caribou WHA 5-116 33 3 80 240

title: Ministerial order for Caribou WHA
summary: The silviculture system is prescribed by ministerial order, specifically group selection harvesting up to 33% of each stand on an 80-year cycle with specific opening size constraints.
source_quote: The silviculture system within that portion of the caribou wildlife habitat area where modified harvesting is allowed is prescribed by ministerial order... The GWM for WHA 5-116 prescribes group selection harvesting up to 33% of each stand by area on an 80-year cutting cycle.

title: 6.1.5.4 Wildlife habitat restoration
summary: Section heading for wildlife habitat restoration assumptions.
source_quote: 6.1.5.4 Wildlife habitat restoration

title: Table 18. Habitat restoration objectives
summary: Lists habitat restoration objectives for Grassland, American Badger, and Great Basin Spadefoot, specifying single entry harvest and 100% post-harvest exclusion.
source_quote: Table 18. Habitat restoration objectives ... Grassland 1 100 ... Badger 1 100 ... Spadefoot 1 100

title: Benchmark Grasslands management
summary: Assumptions for Benchmark Grassland areas include retaining 5-10% as contiguous forested area, maintaining older trees in grassland portions, and specific recruitment tree densities.
source_quote: Maintain modified harvesting as the recommended best management practice within Grassland Benchmark Areas; Maintain 5-10% as contiguous forested area (retention portion); In the 90-95% grassland portion, maintain 90-100% of all trees > 140 years of age.

title: Current Forest Management Assumptions
summary: Section covering current forest management assumptions regarding pests and assumptions for modeling.
source_quote: 6. Current Forest Management Assumptions

title: Current Forest Management Assumptions - Spruce and Balsam Bark Beetles
summary: Spruce and balsam stands comprise about 9% of the TSA. Balsam bark beetle is considered endemic. Spruce bark beetle infestation levels are based on aerial surveys, with an assumed cyclical pattern of 5-10 year infestations occurring every 30 years. Unsalvaged volume is calculated using the revised Henigmann method.
source_quote: Spruce and balsam stands account for about nine percent of the TSA... Spruce beetle infestations are cyclical; therefore volume losses will mainly occur during infestations, but not between them. It is assumed that infestations last 5-10 years, and occur every 30 years.

title: Spruce Beetle Infestation Cycle
summary: It is assumed that spruce beetle infestations last 5-10 years and occur every 30 years.
source_quote: It is assumed that infestations last 5-10 years, and occur every 30 years.

title: Table 19a. Area of infestation for Douglas-fir, Spruce and Balsam bark beetles
summary: Average area of infestation (ha) from 2006-2010 for Douglas-fir (10,470.2 ha), Spruce (7,619.8 ha), and Balsam (10,267.8 ha) bark beetles, broken down by severity (Trace, Light, Moderate, Severe, Very severe).
source_quote: Table 19a. Area of infestation for Douglas-fir, Spruce and Balsam bark beetles (averaged from 2006 – 2010)

title: Tree Mortality Classes
summary: Definitions for tree mortality disturbance types: Trace (<1%), Light (1-10%), Moderate (11-29%), Severe (30-40%), and Very severe (>50%) of trees in polygon killed.
source_quote: Disturbance type Class Description Tree mortaliy ... Trace < 1% of trees in polygon killed ... Light 1-10% ... Moderate 11-29% ... Severe 30-49% ... Very severe > 50%

title: Current Forest Management Assumptions - Spruce Budworm
summary: Western spruce budworm populations declined significantly after aggressive spray programs (2007-2009). Defoliation was 39,000 ha in 2010. Estimated volume loss assumes 2% of moderate/severe areas incur mortality, with mortality volume in Douglas-fir stands assumed at 250m3/ha/year.
source_quote: Following aggressive B.t.k. spray program in 2007, 2008 and 2009 western spruce budworm populations declined significantly in the TSA... Estimated volume loss from spruce budworm is based on the following assumptions: two percent of the average annual moderate and severe area... would incur mortality... mortality volume / ha in Douglas-fir stands is assumed to be = 250m3/year.

title: Spruce Budworm Defoliation Area 2010
summary: The 2010 aerial overview survey found defoliation on just over 39,000 hectares.
source_quote: The 2010 aerial overview survey found defoliation on just over 39 000 hectares

title: Douglas-fir Mortality from Spruce Budworm
summary: Douglas-fir is assumed to die after four consecutive years of severe defoliation by spruce budworm.
source_quote: This is the case with Douglas-fir, the species most vulnerable to spruce budworm attacks, which dies after four consecutive years of severe defoliation

title: Spruce Budworm Mortality Volume
summary: Mortality volume per hectare in Douglas-fir stands is assumed to be 250 m3/year.
source_quote: mortality volume / ha in Douglas-fir stands is assumed to be = 250m3/year

title: Condition of MPB dead pine
summary: Section detailing how lodgepole pine impacted by Mountain Pine Beetle degrades over time.
source_quote: 6.2.2 Condition of MPB dead pine

title: Condition of MPB dead pine
summary: Dead lodgepole pine degrades in quality over time. Shelf life determines commercial viability. It is assumed dead trees remain standing for at least 15 years. Modeling uses Years Since Death (YSD) classes based on the BC Mountain Pine Beetle Model.
source_quote: Lodgepole pine trees impacted by MPB start to degrade upon death... It is assumed that the dead trees will have some commercial use... as long as the trees are standing. Dead trees will be assumed to remain standing for at least 15 years after attack.

title: Standing Dead Pine Shelf Life
summary: Dead trees are assumed to remain standing for at least 15 years after attack.
source_quote: Dead trees will be assumed to remain standing for at least 15 years after attack.

title: 6.4.1 Second generation site index estimates
source_quote: 6.4.1 Second generation site index estimates

title: 6.4.1 Second generation site index estimates
summary: The section describes the use of second-generation SIBEC site index estimates for site productivity in the TIPSY growth model. These estimates, released by FLNR in 2011, offer improved accuracy over first-approximation values and are used with PEM. Implementing SIBEC can potentially increase timber harvesting land base area, redistribute stands to higher site classes, reduce green-up time, and reduce minimum harvest age.
source_quote: Improved site productivity information may be obtained for young and future managed stands based on the BEC information provided by the PEM. ... Second generation SIBEC tables were released by FLNR in 2011 and will be used in conjunction with PEM (see Section 3.3) to estimate site productivity for the TIPSY growth model.

title: Use of Second Generation SIBEC Estimates
summary: The analysis assumes the use of second-generation SIBEC site index estimates released by FLNR in 2011, used in conjunction with PEM for the TIPSY growth model.
source_quote: Second generation SIBEC tables were released by FLNR in 2011 and will be used in conjunction with PEM (see Section 3.3) to estimate site productivity for the TIPSY growth model.

title: 6.5.1 Regeneration activities in managed stands
source_quote: 6.5.1 Regeneration activities in managed stands

title: 6.5.1 Regeneration activities in managed stands
summary: Regeneration assumptions for managed stands are based on Managed Stand Yield Tables (MSYT) produced using TIPSY. Inputs are summarized from RESULTS free-growing survey data for 22,860 hectares since 1999. Regular spacing is assumed for all MSYT. Actual establishment methods are ignored except for genetic gains.
source_quote: Recent plantations and future stands will be grown on managed stand yield tables (MSYT) produced using TIPSY based on observed current practices. The inputs required to produce MSYT shown in Table 21 were summarized from RESULTS free-growing survey data for 22 860 hectares recorded since 1999.

title: Table 21. Regeneration assumptions by analysis unit
summary: Table detailing regeneration assumptions including site index, regeneration delay, species composition, and initial density for various analysis units categorized by species and productivity level.
source_quote: Table 21. Regeneration assumptions by analysis unit ... 11 Decid poor 14 2 At 50% ... 54 Spruce very good 19 1 Pli 40% ...

title: Survey Data Area
summary: The MSYT inputs are based on free-growing survey data for 22,860 hectares.
source_quote: The inputs required to produce MSYT shown in Table 21 were summarized from RESULTS free-growing survey data for 22 860 hectares recorded since 1999.

title: Regular Spacing Assumption
summary: Regular spacing (the 'planted' option in TIPSY) is assumed for all MSYT, with initial density based on well-spaced trees.
source_quote: Regular spacing is assumed (i.e., the “planted” option in TIPSY) for all MSYT so the initial density was based on total well-spaced trees where available, otherwise well-spaced was used.

title: Impact of Establishment Method
summary: Since MSYT are based on stand condition at free-growing, the actual method of stand establishment is not considered except for estimating genetic gains.
source_quote: Since MSYT are based on the stand condition at free growing, the actual method of stand establishment is no longer considered except for estimating genetic gains (see Section 6.5.1.1).

title: OAF Values Assumption
summary: Standard OAF values are assumed to be 15% for OAF1 and 5% for OAF2, except in MPB impacted young pine stands.
source_quote: Standard OAF values of OAF1 - 15% and OAF2 - 5% except in MPB impacted young pine stands (Section 6.3).

title: No Thinning or Fertilization
summary: The model assumes no thinning or fertilization activities.
source_quote: No thinning or fertilization is assumed.

title: Poor Productivity Unit Assumption
summary: Where less than 50 hectares of records were available for poor productivity analysis units, silviculture assumptions from the corresponding medium productivity unit were used.
source_quote: Where less than 50 hectares of records were available, the silviculture assumptions from the corresponding medium productivity analysis unit were used.

title: 6.5.1.1 Genetic gain
source_quote: 6.5.1.1 Genetic gain

title: 6.5.2 Immature plantation history
summary: Section heading for immature plantation history assumptions.
source_quote: 6.5.2 Immature plantation history

title: 6.5.2 Immature plantation history
summary: This section identifies areas of existing immature forest with controlled stocking density to be assigned to TIPSY managed stand yield tables. It defines managed stands, notes that stands over 50 years use natural yield tables, and specifies that young pine stands include Mountain Pine Beetle impacts. Variable retention and group selection systems are modeled on managed tables, while single tree systems use variable retention adjustment factors.
source_quote: The purpose of this section is to identify areas of existing immature forest where stocking density (stems per hectare) was controlled and therefore should be assigned to a TIPSY managed stand yield table.

title: Age threshold for natural yield tables
summary: Stands greater than 50 years old are assumed to be grown using natural stand yield tables rather than managed yield tables.
source_quote: Stands greater than 50 years old will be grown using natural stand yield tables.

title: 6.5.3 Not satisfactorily restocked (NSR) areas
summary: Section heading for Not Satisfactorily Restocked areas.
source_quote: 6.5.3 Not satisfactorily restocked (NSR) areas

title: 6.5.3 Not satisfactorily restocked (NSR) areas
summary: This section identifies NSR areas in the timber harvesting land base and estimates restocking rates. Backlog NSR is based on historical reports, with expectations that 50% will be declared free growing this year and all treated by 2015. Current NSR is based on recent harvesting data and is expected to regenerate per Table 21 and decline as MPB salvage opportunities decrease.
source_quote: The purpose of this section is to identify the total area of NSR currently existing in the timber harvesting land base, and the estimated rate at which the NSR area will be restocked.

title: Backlog NSR restoration timeline
summary: It is claimed that the long-term expectation is for all backlog NSR area to be treated and declared free growing by 2015.
source_quote: The long-term expectation is that all backlog area will be treated and declared by 2015.

title: Table 23. Not satisfactorily restocked (NSR) areas
summary: Table detailing total areas and restocking projections for Backlog NSR and Current NSR across different age ranges.
source_quote: Table 23. Not satisfactorily restocked (NSR) areas

title: Backlog NSR Total Area
summary: The total area of Backlog NSR is 1,132 hectares.
source_quote: Backlog NSR 1,132

title: Current NSR Total Area
summary: The total area of Current NSR is 50,596 hectares.
source_quote: Current NSR 50,596

title: 6.6 Integrated resource management
summary: Section heading for Integrated resource management.
source_quote: 6.6 Integrated resource management

title: 6.6.1 Objectives that limit area disturbed
summary: Section heading for objectives limiting area disturbed.
source_quote: 6.6.1 Objectives that limit area disturbed

title: 6.6.1 Objectives that limit area disturbed
summary: Area disturbance constraints are modeled for IRM zones and visually sensitive areas. IRM zones follow a three-pass harvest regime. Visual quality objectives are established under the CCLUP, with disturbance percentages modeled based on mid-points from visual quality classes. Productive forested area outside the THLB contributes to visual quality but not IRM objectives.
source_quote: Area disturbance constraints will be modelled for the integrated resources management zone and the visually sensitive areas.

title: THLB Maximum Allowable Disturbance
summary: The maximum allowable disturbance for the Timber Harvesting Land Base (THLB) is set at 33%.
source_quote: THLB 33

title: 7. Sensitivity Analyses to be Performed
source_quote: 7. Sensitivity Analyses to be Performed

title: 7. Sensitivity Analyses to be Performed
summary: This section explains the purpose of sensitivity analysis in measuring timber supply impact regarding uncertainty in management assumptions or data integrity. It notes that analysis helps determine which variables most affect results and references Table 27 for standard sensitivity issues.
source_quote: Sensitivity analysis can provide a measure of the timber supply impact if uncertainty in management assumptions and/or data integrity exists. ... By developing and testing a number of sensitivity analyses, it is possible to determine which variables most affect results.

title: Table 27. Sensitivity issues
summary: A table listing standard sensitivity analysis issues and their respective levels, including minimum harvestable age, yield changes, land base changes, and specific forestry factors.
source_quote:
Table 27. Sensitivity issues 
Issue to be tested Sensitivity levels 
Minimum harvestable age + / - 5 yrs 
Existing stand yields + / - 10% 
Regenerated stand yields + / - 10% 
Land base changes + / - 10% 
Visual quality objectives Lower disturbance limits 
PEM/SIBEC Use VRI site index 
Regeneration One year regeneration delay, all blocks planted 
Green-up height + / - 1 m 
Operability Include all class 2 
Deciduous Exclude deciduous leading 
Dead pine fall-over age + / - 5 yrs 
Abandon MPB salvage Harvest in live stands only 
Green-up height + / - 1 m

title: Minimum harvestable age sensitivity
summary: Testing the impact of varying the minimum harvestable age by plus or minus 5 years.
source_quote: Minimum harvestable age + / - 5 yrs

title: Existing stand yields sensitivity
summary: Testing the impact of varying existing stand yields by plus or minus 10%.
source_quote: Existing stand yields + / - 10%

title: Regenerated stand yields sensitivity
summary: Testing the impact of varying regenerated stand yields by plus or minus 10%.
source_quote: Regenerated stand yields + / - 10%

title: Land base changes sensitivity
summary: Testing the impact of land base changes by plus or minus 10%.
source_quote: Land base changes + / - 10%

title: Visual quality objectives sensitivity
summary: Testing the impact of applying lower disturbance limits to visual quality objectives.
source_quote: Visual quality objectives Lower disturbance limits

title: PEM/SIBEC site index sensitivity
summary: Testing the impact of using VRI site index for PEM/SIBEC.
source_quote: PEM/SIBEC Use VRI site index

title: Regeneration delay sensitivity
summary: Testing the impact of a one-year regeneration delay with all blocks planted.
source_quote: Regeneration One year regeneration delay, all blocks planted

title: Green-up height sensitivity
summary: Testing the impact of varying green-up height by plus or minus 1 meter.
source_quote: Green-up height + / - 1 m

title: Operability sensitivity
summary: Testing the impact of including all class 2 areas in operability analysis.
source_quote: Operability Include all class 2

title: Deciduous exclusion sensitivity
summary: Testing the impact of excluding deciduous leading from the analysis.
source_quote: Deciduous Exclude deciduous leading

title: Dead pine fall-over age sensitivity
summary: Testing the impact of varying dead pine fall-over age by plus or minus 5 years.
source_quote: Dead pine fall-over age + / - 5 yrs
